# Questions in Livestream 129

Live Stream #129: Anything But Ammonites

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=350" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:50</a> | What are the origins and meanings of the names Aaron and Miriam? | Miriam is treated as an Egyptian-derived name; Aaron is harder to explain and the answer is more tentative. | He says Miriam is straightforwardly Egyptian, deriving it from an Egyptian word/name meaning beloved or to love, with the final Hebrew mem added because Hebrew dislikes names ending in a vowel. Aaron is much less certain: he offers an Egyptian candidate meaning something like spirit of men, but stresses that several Egyptian precursors are possible and that the etymology remains a tentative best guess. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=593" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:53</a> | Did Queen Merneith reign as a proto-pharaoh? | She was an important First Dynasty woman, but whether she truly reigned is uncertain. | He identifies Merneith as a First Dynasty figure, probably connected to kings as daughter, wife, and mother, and notes that she held administrative authority beyond ordinary royal-woman status. Her own serekh-like monument suggests unusual power, perhaps enough to raise the proto-pharaoh question, but he does not present her reign as certain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:10</a> | Would Jephthah have considered Yahweh just like any other Canaanite god, hence his sacrifice? | No; the answer distinguishes Jephthah's vow from simply treating Yahweh as another local god. | He says the text does not let us know exactly what Jephthah believed about Yahweh, but Jephthah's practice looks Canaanite even if his belief was not formally Canaanite. In his speech to Ammon, Jephthah seems to put Yahweh and the Ammonite god on a comparable tier as land-granting deities, so the failure is real but hard to classify precisely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=994" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:34</a> | What is your opinion of Martin Luther, and was he fair despite harsh comments about Jews? | Luther is treated as complicated: important as a reformer, but not excused for his anti-Jewish writings. | He describes Luther as a harsh, foul-mouthed Augustinian monk who attacked many groups, including Jews, Catholics, Anabaptists, monks, and even his own religious background. He still sees Luther as someone God used in a corrupt historical moment to move the church toward greater orthodoxy, while plainly rejecting Luther's anti-Jewish comments and noting other serious flaws and mistakes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=1907" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:47</a> | Why didn't Upper Egypt sabotage the Bahr Yussef to prevent Delta flooding control and hurt the Hyksos? | The canal was not called that in antiquity, and the answer gives practical and political reasons sabotage would not work that way. | He first corrects the anachronistic name, saying the canal was the ancient Egyptian Maywer, not Bahr Yussef. He then argues sabotage would have been impractical because the canal was large, deep, and wide, and because the late Thirteenth Dynasty was collapsing under Hyksos pressure; the court fled south quickly and did not have the time or control needed for major sabotage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=2188" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:28</a> | Do you still stand by your previous assessment of the Hammurabi-Abraham synchronism? | Yes; he still thinks the synchronism is a useful working hypothesis because of both date and historical similarities. | He says he still accepts the synchronism because the transliteration issue is not decisive against it and the proposal has appeared in scholarship before. He also thinks the historical setting works: the relevant Elamite kings have possible candidates in Hammurabi's period, and the synchronism fits related links with Terah, so he treats it as a strong working hypothesis rather than merely a similar name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=2276" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:56</a> | Can you explain high and low chronology, where they come from, and the current consensus? | High and low chronology differ over how Egyptian and related ancient dates are synchronized; he says low chronology has broadly won in principle though details remain disputed. | He contrasts high chronology, which relies heavily on archaeoastronomy such as the Sothic cycle, with low chronology, which prioritizes synchronisms such as the Amarna letters. He favors low or low-middle chronology because synchronisms are harder data, says high chronology is now effectively defunct in the field, and adds that scholars still argue over the exact low-chronology details. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=3983" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:23</a> | Do people have Ebenezer Scrooge moments with God in real life, resulting in a 180-degree change? | Rarely; Saul of Tarsus is the kind of example, but such dramatic turnarounds are uncommon. | He says dramatic reversals can happen, with Saul of Tarsus as the biblical example, but they are rare and not simple instant fixes. Even Paul had consequences and a long period before ministry under Barnabas, so the more common response to a confrontation with God is rejection rather than immediate transformation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=4112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:32</a> | If only the tablets remained in the ark in 1 Kings 8:9, how were Aaron's staff and the manna removed without killing anyone? | The transcript says the Bible never states they were placed inside the ark; the wording allows them to have been beside it. | He reads 1 Kings 8:9 as consistent with the rest of the biblical wording: only the stone tablets are explicitly placed inside the ark, while Aaron's staff, the manna, the red-heifer ash, and the book of the law are described as placed before it. He suggests those items may have traveled in another container and been set before the ark, and adds that ritual handling of holy objects was possible under proper conditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=4589" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:29</a> | What are your thoughts on entire sanctification? | He calls Christian perfectionism a confusing term and rejects the idea that believers become sinless in this life. | He distinguishes Wesley's idea from a distorted claim that Christians cannot sin at all. He agrees that salvation changes a believer's desires, that sin is no longer counted against the believer in Christ, and that Christians should sin less, but he rejects a strong perfectionist reading because Christians still sin and still need confession. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=5143" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:43</a> | Do you have a solution to secularism, occultism, and hedonism in Western culture? | Get back to Scripture, biblical literacy, church, and healthy Christian community. | He answers that the solution is not a single program but a return to Scripture, church, sound doctrine, prayer, Bible reading, Christian meditation, charity, service, and use of spiritual gifts in the body. He emphasizes biblical literacy in the home and church, arguing that shallow faith and failure to teach Scripture are major reasons Western Christian culture is vulnerable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=5780" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:20</a> | When did the ancient Near East use naval warfare, and how did it function in the Bronze Age? | Egypt had a navy, but he says it was more important for transport and supply routes than for offensive naval warfare. | He says Egypt had a navy, but its main Bronze Age function was supplying armies that moved by land along the Mediterranean coast and into Canaan. Some ships had defensive military features such as archery towers and guarded ports, but he presents them chiefly as cargo and support vessels; Israel was not seafaring, and Philistine naval details are less clear. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=6034" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:34</a> | What were the two offenses of Aaron's sons? | He identifies removing the ark poles and introducing strange fire from outside the Tabernacle precinct. | He says the first offense was removing the poles from the ark, which were supposed to remain in the rings. The second was bringing in strange fire, likely fire of unknown or ritually improper origin from outside the Tabernacle precinct, rather than fire prepared with ritually clean materials inside the sacred area. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=6201" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:21</a> | Are Palestinians descendants of Ishmael, or could they be descendants of Jews? | He says neither; he treats them as Arabic Semitic tribes rather than biblical Ishmaelites or Jews. | He rejects both proposed genealogies, saying the claim that Arabs or Palestinians descend from Ishmael is Islamic theology rather than biblical theology. He instead describes them as Arabic Semitic tribes, probably moving west from the eastern Arabian Peninsula around the tenth century BC and displacing earlier Ishmaelites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=6422" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:02</a> | Was there an Egyptian intellectual or theological influence during the formation of the doctrine of the Trinity? | He says the New Testament is the key influence, not an Egyptian intellectual movement. | He says the doctrine of the Trinity comes from reconciling New Testament and Israelite claims: God is one, yet the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are each spoken of as God. He notes broader intellectual currents such as Neoplatonism, but does not think they drove the doctrine, and points to an early second-century reference from Antioch rather than Egypt as evidence that it was not Egyptian in origin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=6753" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:33</a> | What ethnicity was Noah, and what do you think of Irving Finkel's ark reconstruction from Babylonian tablets? | Noah's ethnicity is not knowable from the text; he thinks Finkel is a good Assyriologist but misses the mark on the ark reconstruction. | He says Noah is from Seth's line, but ethnicity as a later group concept is not presented before the flood, so Noah is better treated as progenitor than as a member of a known ethnicity. On Finkel, he respects him as an Assyriologist and sees the reconstruction as an interesting thought experiment, but thinks Genesis describes the ark as a chest or box tied to temple and sacred-space themes, not the round Mesopotamian-style vessel Finkel reconstructs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=7617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:57</a> | Do you agree that concupiscence is itself sinful? | No; he does not think it is inherently sinful, though he notes some Reformers disagreed. | He defines concupiscence as a strong desire or temptation toward sin and says some classical Protestant traditions treat it as sinful. He disagrees, arguing that sin requires a deed, an act of will, or an act of thought; the desire or temptation itself can be resisted, but choosing to follow it becomes sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=7853" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:53</a> | What do you think of Machiavelli's claim that if Moses had been unarmed, he could not have enforced his constitutions? | He says Machiavelli is right as a pragmatist, while noting The Prince is not a warm book. | He characterizes Machiavelli as a pragmatist writing advice for rulers and says the statement about Moses is basically correct. Israel may have left Egypt unarmed, but they quickly turned bronze into weapons and fought Amalek, so he thinks the wilderness community would not have survived without weapons of some kind. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=8471" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:11</a> | How were people saved in the Old Testament? | By faith, as with Abraham; he rejects a dispensational split in the basic way of salvation. | He says Abraham was saved by faith counted as righteousness, and that Old Testament people were saved by faith in Yahweh rather than by law-keeping itself. The sacrificial system covered sin for relationship with God, but obedience was the fruit of faith and love, not the thing that saved; he rejects a dispensational view that makes the saving mechanism fundamentally different in each era. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=9529" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:38:49</a> | What was the role of Assyrians in the Near East during Joshua's conquest? | At that point Assyria was not the later empire; he places them in an earlier, more limited Mesopotamian context. | He places Joshua's conquest around 1215-1220 BC, in the early portion of the Middle Assyrian Empire, long before the Neo-Assyrians. Assyria was expanding through Mesopotamia, but Mitanni and the Hittites stood between them and Canaan, so their role in Joshua's region was limited compared with later imperial Assyria. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=9805" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:25</a> | Do Neanderthals and other groups descend from Adam? | He thinks so, though he presents it as personal opinion and notes different ways of reading Genesis. | He says his personal view is that major hominid groups such as Neanderthals are human because they show human traits like burials, grave goods, religious awareness, and cave art. Whether they descend specifically from Adam depends on how one reads Genesis 1 and Adam's role, so he leaves room for either direct descent from Adam or descent from the first humans with Adam later serving as representative high priest. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=10185" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:45</a> | In Numbers 16:30-34, was the ground opening an earthquake or another natural phenomenon, and what is happening with the incense and plague in verses 41-50? | He treats the earth opening as a miraculous judgment and explains the incense as functioning like a sin offering that stops the plague. | He says the ground opening looks more like a sinkhole or collapsed underground cavern than a movie-style earthquake fault, though the timing marks it as divine judgment. For the later plague, he says the specific disease is not named, and Aaron's incense functions as an expensive atonement or sin-offering rite that stops the plague among the congregation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=10595" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:35</a> | Which is more beneficial for Christians to read: the Apocrypha, Gnostic texts, or early church fathers? | He recommends the early church fathers, while warning they are not Scripture and must be read critically. | He ranks the early church fathers first because they contain valuable theological reflection and train careful reading, especially in the first few centuries. He still warns that they are diverse and not Scripture; he finds the Apocrypha useful as New Testament context but less valuable, and treats Gnostic texts as least useful because they mainly serve allegorical and secret-knowledge agendas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=10872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:12</a> | Is Maccabees useful? | Second Maccabees is more useful than First Maccabees for the Maccabean War, though he still urges caution. | He says Second Maccabees is more useful than First Maccabees for the Maccabean War because there are few texts for that period, but he is still lukewarm on Maccabees overall. In the same discussion he says he would put the early church fathers ahead of both Maccabees and other Apocrypha for Christian benefit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=11520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:00</a> | Do you know of mainstream Egyptologists who support the Book of Mormon? | He says very few, mostly people connected to BYU. | He says nearly all Egyptologists who support the Book of Mormon are connected with Brigham Young University, naming John Gee and Crystal Pierce as examples, and frames such support as tied to Mormon institutional context. His broader claim is that non-Mormon mainstream Egyptology does not support the Book of Mormon, especially where Book of Abraham material is identified with ordinary Egyptian funerary scenes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=11741" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:41</a> | Is sacred space only where God chooses to reveal himself and act? | No; he says that is not the biblical or ancient Near Eastern definition of sacred space. | He defines sacred space as the space where a deity resides, not merely where God reveals himself or acts. He argues that God is transcendent from profane space, discusses the Eastern Orthodox distinction between essence and energies as a useful analogy, and rejects a simple omnipresence definition because it blurs the biblical separation between sacred and profane. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=12207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:27</a> | Where would you put Ken Ham among dangerous or problematic teachers? | He calls some of Ham's views problematic, but puts the Hovinds and many word-faith teachers in a more dangerous category. | He says he would not put Ken Ham in the most dangerous category, though some of Ham's views are problematic. He places figures like Kent and Eric Hovind, Joel Osteen, Kenneth Copeland, and Creflo Dollar in a more damaging category, then ties the larger problem to weak biblical literacy and putting Christian leaders on pedestals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=12584" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:44</a> | What were the Magi? | He identifies the Magi as a tribal group from Kush. | He describes the Magi as a tribal group from Kush, around the region between Nubia and Thebes, with a native area near Aswan. According to his answer, they cooperated with Egypt, became trusted mercenaries and constables, played both sides during the Hyksos conflict, and were treated as unusually trusted foreigners with movement privileges. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=12805" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:25</a> | Any thoughts on Michael Pro? | He says the figure is associated with a gnostic-style cult and treats it as spiritually dangerous. | He says Michael Pro's Valley of Healing group is essentially Gnostic, citing claims to secret saving knowledge, spirit messages, allegorical Bible readings, levels of angels and gods, and the idea that flesh is evil. He calls the group dangerous and advises staying away from it, while noting that Sentinel Apologetics has done work on the topic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=13148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:08</a> | Should church buildings be considered sacred spaces? | Generally no; he says churches should be respected, but calling them sacred spaces misses the New Testament movement of the Spirit into the world. | He says churches are not temples and generally should not be called sacred spaces in the biblical sense. The crucifixion and resurrection, especially the torn temple veil and the Spirit going out into the world, mean sacred presence is no longer boxed into a temple space, though church buildings should still be treated respectfully. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=13354" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:34</a> | Is "on earth as it is in heaven" an invitation to sanctify more and more of the earth? | Yes; he says the kingdom of Christ pushes the sacred outward into the world through holiness and love. | He agrees that the phrase invites the sanctification of more and more of the earth. He frames Christ's kingdom as sacred presence moving outward into every space of the world, not by violence or by creating isolated sacred enclaves, but through love, holiness, sanctification, and the calling to be a holy people. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=13872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:12</a> | What are your thoughts on the modern state of Israel and the prophecy of Zechariah 12:1-9? | He reads the passage as about Israel specifically, while keeping the answer focused on the text. | After reading Zechariah 12:1-9, he says God is not done with Israel and that Israel remains part of God's plan and the sons of promise. He also says modern Israel still needs salvation in Jesus Christ, supports evangelistic ministries to Jewish people, and urges prayer for Jerusalem, Israel's peace, and Jewish salvation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/FLegx9MGmdc?t=14239" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:19</a> | How related are modern Israelites to ancient Israelites, and what is the difference between ancient Hebrew and modern Hebrew? | Modern Hebrew is reconstructed and differs from ancient Hebrew; he treats modern Jewish descent as related but complex. | He says modern Hebrew is a reconstructed language, distinct from ancient and medieval Hebrew, with simplifications, changed grammar, lost forms, and new meanings for old words. On descent, he says modern Jewish communities preserved identity but experienced much intermarriage and genetic change, so relation to ancient Israelites is real but complex rather than genetically simple. |
